text_a: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) must be enforced on all cloud accounts as the primary defense against credential compromise.
text_b: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) must be enforced for all privileged and administrative accounts on all systems, with no exceptions.
explanation: Both obligations mandate MFA, but POL‑002 §5.2 applies to all cloud accounts while POL‑013 §3.1 covers privileged and admin accounts on all systems. The broader cloud‑wide requirement and the more specific privileged‑account requirement reinforce each other without conflict.

text_a: All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or stronger.
text_b: All cloud object and block storage containing Confidential or Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 encryption.
explanation: POL‑009 §3.1 requires AES‑256 encryption at rest for all confidential/restricted data, whereas POL‑010 §4.1 requires the same encryption for cloud object or block storage that holds such data. The latter is a narrower, environment‑specific subset that supports the broader policy.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: CloudTrail (or equivalent audit logging) must be enabled at all times on every account and region.
text_b: Audit logging (including CloudTrail or equivalent) must remain enabled at all times on all production systems.
explanation: POL‑002 §5.3 mandates that CloudTrail (or equivalent) is always enabled on every cloud account and region, while POL‑011 §3.1 requires audit logging to remain enabled on all production systems. The scopes overlap but are not contradictory; each requirement reinforces the other.

text_a: All cloud-hosted data stores must use AES-256 encryption at rest.
text_b: All cloud object and block storage containing Confidential or Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 encryption.
explanation: POL‑002 §5.1 requires AES‑256 encryption at rest for all cloud‑hosted data stores; POL‑010 §4.1 applies only to cloud object/block storage containing confidential or restricted data, also requiring AES‑256 at rest. The more specific rule supplements the broader one without conflict.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using industry-standard encryption (minimum AES-256) and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
text_b: All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or stronger.
explanation: Both policies address confidential/restricted data encryption at rest with AES‑256 or stronger. POL‑004 §3.2 includes the same rest‑encryption requirement (plus transit), making POL‑009 §3.1’s rest requirement redundant.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using industry-standard encryption (minimum AES-256) and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
text_b: All cloud object and block storage containing Confidential or Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 encryption.
explanation: POL‑004 §3.2 requires AES‑256 at rest for all confidential/restricted data, whereas POL‑010 §4.1 restricts the requirement to cloud object/block storage. The cloud‑specific rule complements the broader data‑classification rule without contradiction.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or stronger.
text_b: Backup media must be encrypted at rest.
explanation: POL-009 §3.1 requires AES‑256 encryption for Confidential and Restricted data at rest, while POL-016 §3.3 requires encryption of all backup media at rest. The two obligations apply to different scopes (data vs. backup media) and reinforce overall encryption controls.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: All employees must rotate passwords every 90 days for all corporate accounts, including cloud-hosted systems.
text_b: Forced password rotation is deprecated as a control in favor of MFA and will not be required for cloud-hosted systems.
explanation: POL-001 §3.1 mandates 90‑day password rotation for all corporate accounts, including cloud‑hosted systems, whereas POL-002 §5.2 deprecates forced rotation for cloud‑hosted systems, creating a direct contradiction.

text_a: Logging must not be disabled outside of an approved, time-boxed exception under the Incident Response Policy.
text_b: Logging must not be manually disabled by an engineer for debugging or any other ad hoc purpose.
explanation: POL-002 §5.3 allows logging to be disabled only under an approved Incident Response exception, while POL-011 §3.1 prohibits engineers from disabling logging for debugging or ad‑hoc purposes. The exception in POL-002 is a formal, non‑ad‑hoc process, thus the obligations coexist.

text_a: All cloud-hosted data stores must use AES-256 encryption at rest.
text_b: All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or stronger.
explanation: POL-002 §5.1 requires AES‑256 encryption at rest for all cloud‑hosted data stores, whereas POL-009 §3.1 requires AES‑256 encryption at rest for all Confidential and Restricted data regardless of location. The policies cover different scopes but reinforce encryption requirements.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) must be enforced for all privileged and administrative accounts on all systems, with no exceptions.
text_b: The legacy administrative console for on-prem systems may be accessed using a strong password alone; MFA is not required for these consoles due to compatibility limitations with the legacy authentication module.
explanation: POL-013 §3.1 mandates MFA for all privileged and administrative accounts on all systems with no exceptions, while POL-014 §3.1 permits access to legacy administrative consoles without MFA. The two obligations directly contradict one another for the same type of accounts.

text_a: All cloud object and block storage containing Confidential or Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 encryption.
text_b: Backup media must be encrypted at rest.
explanation: POL-010 §4.1 requires AES‑256 encryption at rest for cloud storage containing Confidential or Restricted data, and POL-016 §3.3 requires encryption of all backup media at rest. The requirements apply to related but distinct scopes and reinforce encryption controls.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: Employees must report suspected security incidents to the IT Helpdesk within 24 hours of discovery
text_b: All confirmed incidents affecting Confidential or Restricted data must be reported to Legal and the DPO within 24 hours of confirmation.
explanation: POL-003 §3.4 mandates incident reporting to IT Helpdesk within 24 hrs for all employees, while POL-012 §5.2 adds a parallel reporting requirement to Legal and DPO for confirmed incidents involving Confidential or Restricted data. The second obligation extends the first for a specific data class and confirmation status, creating an exception rather than a contradiction.

text_a: Security groups and equivalent network controls must default to deny-all inbound, with exceptions requiring documented business justification and expiration date.
text_b: All inbound firewall rules must be reviewed quarterly and documented with business justification.
explanation: POL-002 §5.4 requires security groups to default deny inbound and allows exceptions only with documented justification and expiration. POL-007 §3.3 requires quarterly review and business justification for all inbound firewall rules. The policies reinforce one another by providing documentation requirements, but they do not conflict.

text_a: All cloud-hosted data stores must use AES-256 encryption at rest.
text_b: Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using industry-standard encryption (minimum AES-256) and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
explanation: POL-002 §5.1 mandates AES‑256 encryption at rest for all cloud‑hosted data stores, whereas POL-004 §3.2 requires encryption at rest (minimum AES‑256) for Confidential or Restricted data in any location, adding a TLS 1.2+ requirement for transit. The two obligations apply to overlapping data sets but one is broader; they support each other rather than conflict.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: All production systems must maintain a daily on-premises backup in addition to any other backup mechanism, retained for a minimum of 30 days.
text_b: Cloud-hosted systems using managed multi-region replication are exempt from the on-premises backup requirement defined in the Data Backup Policy, provided replication is verified quarterly and covers all Restricted-tier data.
explanation: POL‑016 §3.1 imposes a daily on‑premises backup for all production systems, whereas POL‑017 §4.1 exempts cloud‑hosted systems that use verified multi‑region replication covering Restricted data. The latter policy explicitly creates an exception to the former, making the relationship complementary.

text_a: All employees must rotate passwords every 90 days for all corporate accounts, including cloud-hosted systems.
text_b: Cloud accounts shall not require periodic password rotation.
explanation: POL‑001 §3.1 requires all employees to rotate passwords every 90 days for all corporate accounts, including cloud‑hosted systems, while POL‑002 §5.2 prohibits periodic password rotation for cloud accounts. Both obligations target the same entities with opposing requirements, and no exception clause reconciles them.

text_a: Security and access audit logs must be retained for a minimum of 3 years.
text_b: Personal data must not be retained longer than necessary for the purpose for which it was collected.
explanation: POL‑005 §3.2 mandates retention of audit logs for at least 3 years, whereas POL‑006 §4.2 prohibits retention of personal data beyond what is necessary. Since the scope of audit logs is not explicitly tied to personal data in these provisions, the obligations are not clearly overlapping or contradictory.

text_a: Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using industry-standard encryption (minimum AES-256) and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
text_b: All data in transit must use TLS 1.2 or higher.
explanation: POL-004 §3.2 mandates TLS 1.2+ for confidential data in transit, while POL-009 §3.2 requires TLS 1.2+ for all in‑transit data. The broader requirement complements the specific one.

text_a: All web traffic should be encrypted using TLS 1.0 or higher when accessing corporate resources remotely
text_b: Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using industry-standard encryption (minimum AES-256) and in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
explanation: POL-003 §3.2 recommends TLS 1.0+ for all remote web traffic, whereas POL-004 §3.2 requires TLS 1.2+ for confidential data in transit. The stricter mandatory requirement supersedes the recommendation without contradiction.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity)

text_a: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) must be enforced on all cloud accounts as the primary defense against credential compromise.
text_b: The legacy administrative console for on-prem systems may be accessed using a strong password alone; MFA is not required for these consoles due to compatibility limitations with the legacy authentication module.
explanation: POL-002 §5.2 applies to cloud accounts, while POL-014 §3.1 concerns legacy on‑prem administrative consoles. Their scopes are distinct, so the obligations do not interact.

text_a: Security and access audit logs must be retained for a minimum of 3 years.
text_b: Log data must be retained for a minimum of 1 year in an immutable store.
explanation: POL-005 §3.2 requires audit logs to be retained for 3 years, and POL-011 §3.2 requires all logs to be retained for at least 1 year. The stricter 3‑year rule applies to audit logs, complementing the general requirement.

text_a: Personal data must not be retained longer than necessary for the purpose for which it was collected.
text_b: Log data must be retained for a minimum of 1 year in an immutable store.
explanation: POL-006 §4.2 prohibits retaining personal data longer than necessary; POL-011 §3.2 mandates a minimum 1‑year retention for logs, which may contain personal data for EU subjects, creating an irreconcilable conflict.

text_a: All production systems must maintain a daily on-premises backup in addition to any other backup mechanism, retained for a minimum of 30 days.
text_b: Cloud-hosted systems using managed multi-region replication are exempt from the on-premises backup requirement defined in the Data Backup Policy, provided replication is verified quarterly and covers all Restricted-tier data.
explanation: POL-016 §3.1 requires daily on‑prem backups for all production systems, but POL-017 §4.1 exempts cloud‑hosted systems when quarterly replicated multi‑region data covers Restricted tier. The exception complements the blanket requirement.

text_a: All cloud-hosted data stores must use AES-256 encryption at rest.
text_b: Backup media must be encrypted at rest.
explanation: POL‑002 §5.1 requires AES‑256 at rest for cloud data stores, while POL‑016 §3.3 mandates encryption of backup media. They cover distinct objects (data stores vs backup media) and reinforce overall data‑at‑rest security.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: Employees should use strong passwords
text_b: All employees must use the corporate VPN when accessing internal systems remotely.
explanation: POL‑003 §3.1 recommends strong passwords for employees; POL‑007 §3.1 mandates VPN use when accessing internal systems remotely. The obligations target different behaviors (password policy vs remote access) with no overlapping scope.

text_a: Financial and transactional records must be retained for a minimum of 7 years from the date of creation, in accordance with SOX requirements.
text_b: Personal data must not be retained longer than necessary for the purpose for which it was collected.
explanation: POL‑005 §3.1 governs retention of financial and transactional records (SOX), whereas POL‑006 §4.2 prohibits retention of personal data beyond necessity. The scopes (financial_records vs eu_data_subjects) address different data types and do not conflict.

text_a: Forced password rotation is deprecated as a control in favor of MFA and will not be required for cloud-hosted systems.
text_b: The legacy administrative console for on-prem systems may be accessed using a strong password alone; MFA is not required for these consoles due to compatibility limitations with the legacy authentication module.
explanation: POL‑002 §5.2 prohibits forced password rotation for cloud‑hosted systems, while POL‑014 §3.1 allows password‑only access to legacy admin consoles. They apply to distinct environments (cloud vs on‑prem legacy) and are not contradictory.

text_a: All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or stronger.
text_b: Vendor security posture must be reassessed annually for vendors with access to Confidential or Restricted data.
explanation: POL‑009 §3.1 mandates AES‑256 encryption at rest for Confidential/Restricted data, and POL‑015 §3.3 requires annual vendor reassessment for vendors handling such data. Both obligations protect the same data set but in complementary ways.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: All web traffic should be encrypted using TLS 1.0 or higher when accessing corporate resources remotely
text_b: All data in transit must use TLS 1.2 or higher.
explanation: POL‑003 §3.2 recommends TLS 1.0+ for web traffic to corporate resources remotely, while POL‑009 §3.2 mandates TLS 1.2+ for all data in transit. The recommendation is subsumed by the stronger mandatory requirement, so they coexist without conflict.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity)

text_a: Data must be classified as Public, Internal, Confidential, or Restricted.
text_b: All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or stronger.
explanation: POL-004 §3.1 mandates classification of all data, while POL-009 §3.1 applies encryption only to Confidential and Restricted data, thus the encryption rule is scoped to a subset and complements the classification requirement.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: Security and access audit logs must be retained for a minimum of 3 years.
text_b: All production systems must maintain a daily on-premises backup in addition to any other backup mechanism, retained for a minimum of 30 days.
explanation: POL-005 §3.2 concerns retention of audit logs for 3 years, whereas POL-016 §3.1 requires daily backups for production systems retained 30 days; the scopes (audit logs vs production systems) do not intersect.

text_a: All inbound firewall rules must be reviewed quarterly and documented with business justification.
text_b: Access must be granted according to least-privilege principles and reviewed quarterly.
explanation: POL-007 §3.3 addresses inbound firewall rule review, while POL-013 §3.2 deals with account least‑privilege review; they target distinct objects (firewall rules vs accounts) and have no overlapping scope.

text_a: All inbound firewall rules must be reviewed quarterly and documented with business justification.
text_b: Access must be granted according to least-privilege principles and reviewed quarterly.
explanation: Both obligations involve quarterly review, but one applies to firewall rules and the other to all accounts; the scopes are disjoint, so they are unrelated.

text_a: All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or stronger.
text_b: All confirmed incidents affecting Confidential or Restricted data must be reported to Legal and the DPO within 24 hours of confirmation.
explanation: POL-009 §3.1 encrypts Confidential/Restricted data at rest, while POL-012 §5.2 requires incident reporting for incidents involving such data; the policies address different aspects of the same data categories and therefore complement each other.
controls: NIST SP 800-53 SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)

text_a: Financial and transactional records must be retained for a minimum of 7 years from the date of creation, in accordance with SOX requirements.
text_b: Log data must be retained for a minimum of 1 year in an immutable store.
explanation: POL-005 §3.1 mandates 7‑year retention for financial records under SOX, whereas POL-011 §3.2 requires 1‑year immutable retention for logs from production systems; the scopes (financial records vs production system logs) do not overlap.

text_a: Log data must be retained for a minimum of 1 year in an immutable store.
text_b: All production systems must maintain a daily on-premises backup in addition to any other backup mechanism, retained for a minimum of 30 days.
explanation: Both obligations are mandatory and concern data retention but apply to distinct data sets: POL-011-3.2-o1 mandates log data retention for 1 year, while POL-016-3.1-o2 requires daily backups of all production systems for 30 days. There is no contradiction; they coexist by design.

text_a: Logging must not be disabled outside of an approved, time-boxed exception under the Incident Response Policy.
text_b: During an active, declared security incident, the Incident Commander may authorize temporary suspension of specific logging or monitoring controls for a maximum of 4 hours if required for forensic isolation, provided the exception is documented in the incident ticket and reviewed within 24 hours by the CISO.
explanation: POL-002-5.3-o2 prohibits disabling logging except under an approved, time‑boxed incident‑response exception; POL-012-5.1-o1 authorizes a temporary logging suspension during an incident, which satisfies the exception clause. The two obligations are therefore complementary.

text_a: Financial and transactional records must be retained for a minimum of 7 years from the date of creation, in accordance with SOX requirements.
text_b: All production systems must maintain a daily on-premises backup in addition to any other backup mechanism, retained for a minimum of 30 days.
explanation: POL-005-3.1-o1 governs retention of financial records for 7 years, whereas POL-016-3.1-o2 governs daily backups of all production systems for 30 days. The scopes and data types differ, with no overlapping requirement or contradiction.

text_a: All production systems must maintain a daily on-premises backup in addition to any other backup mechanism, retained for a minimum of 30 days.
text_b: Cloud systems must meet a Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of 1 hour and Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of 4 hours.
explanation: POL-016-3.1-o1 mandates daily backups retained 30 days for all production systems, while POL-017-4.2-o2 specifies RPO/RTO targets for cloud systems. Both requirements support data resilience but do not conflict; they complement each other.

text_a: CloudTrail (or equivalent audit logging) must be enabled at all times on every account and region.
text_b: Logging must not be manually disabled by an engineer for debugging or any other ad hoc purpose.
explanation: POL-002-5.3-o1 requires CloudTrail logging always enabled for all accounts, and POL-011-3.1-o2 prohibits engineers from manually disabling logging. The two mandates reinforce the same objective, making them complementary.

text_a: Personal data must not be retained longer than necessary for the purpose for which it was collected.
text_b: All production systems must maintain a daily on-premises backup in addition to any other backup mechanism, retained for a minimum of 30 days.
explanation: POL-006-4.2-o1 prohibits retaining personal EU data longer than necessary, while POL-016-3.1-o2 requires daily backups of all production systems retained for 30 days. If backups contain EU personal data, they would be retained beyond the 'necessary' period, violating POL-006-4.2-o1; no explicit exception resolves this.

text_a: Logging must not be disabled outside of an approved, time-boxed exception under the Incident Response Policy.
text_b: Audit logging (including CloudTrail or equivalent) must remain enabled at all times on all production systems.
explanation: POL-002 §5.3 (A) permits disabling logging only with an approved Incident Response exception, whereas POL-011 §3.1 (B) mandates logging on all production systems at all times with no exception. Because production systems fall under cloud accounts and regions, the two obligations conflict.

text_a: Logging must not be disabled outside of an approved, time-boxed exception under the Incident Response Policy.
text_b: All CI/CD pipeline traffic exempted under this policy must be logged and reviewed weekly by the Platform Security team.
explanation: POL-002 §5.3 (A) prohibits disabling logging except under an IRP exception, while POL-008 §2.2 (B) requires CI/CD traffic to be logged and reviewed weekly. The logging requirement in (B) is reinforced by (A), making the obligations complementary.

text_a: All CI/CD pipeline traffic exempted under this policy must be logged and reviewed weekly by the Platform Security team.
text_b: During an active, declared security incident, the Incident Commander may authorize temporary suspension of specific logging or monitoring controls for a maximum of 4 hours if required for forensic isolation, provided the exception is documented in the incident ticket and reviewed within 24 hours by the CISO.
explanation: POL-008 §2.2 (A) mandates weekly logging of CI/CD pipeline traffic, whereas POL-012 §5.1 (B) allows temporary suspension of logging for up to 4 hours during an incident with documentation. The exception in (B) provides a controlled override, making the pair complementary.

text_a: CloudTrail (or equivalent audit logging) must be enabled at all times on every account and region.
text_b: During an active, declared security incident, the Incident Commander may authorize temporary suspension of specific logging or monitoring controls for a maximum of 4 hours if required for forensic isolation, provided the exception is documented in the incident ticket and reviewed within 24 hours by the CISO.
explanation: POL-002 §5.3 (A) requires CloudTrail to be enabled at all times, while POL-012 §5.1 (B) permits a 4‑hour suspension during a declared incident when documented. The explicit incident‑based exception in (B) complements the mandatory rule in (A).

text_a: Logging must not be manually disabled by an engineer for debugging or any other ad hoc purpose.
text_b: During an active, declared security incident, the Incident Commander may authorize temporary suspension of specific logging or monitoring controls for a maximum of 4 hours if required for forensic isolation, provided the exception is documented in the incident ticket and reviewed within 24 hours by the CISO.
explanation: POL-011 §3.1 (A) forbids engineers from manually disabling logging for debugging, whereas POL-012 §5.1 (B) allows the Incident Commander to suspend logging for up to 4 hours with proper documentation. The exception is scoped to the Incident Commander, so the two obligations are complementary.

text_a: Audit logging (including CloudTrail or equivalent) must remain enabled at all times on all production systems.
text_b: During an active, declared security incident, the Incident Commander may authorize temporary suspension of specific logging or monitoring controls for a maximum of 4 hours if required for forensic isolation, provided the exception is documented in the incident ticket and reviewed within 24 hours by the CISO.
explanation: POL-011 §3.1 (A) mandates audit logging on all production systems at all times, while POL-012 §5.1 (B) permits temporary suspension of logging during an incident with documentation. The incident‑based exception in (B) complements (A) rather than conflicting.

text_a: CloudTrail (or equivalent audit logging) must be enabled at all times on every account and region.
text_b: All CI/CD pipeline traffic exempted under this policy must be logged and reviewed weekly by the Platform Security team.
explanation: POL‑002 §5.3 mandates CloudTrail logging for all accounts/regions, while POL‑008 §2.2 requires weekly logging and review of CI/CD pipeline traffic. Both requirements coexist without overlapping scope, thereby complementing each other.

text_a: All CI/CD pipeline traffic exempted under this policy must be logged and reviewed weekly by the Platform Security team.
text_b: Logging must not be manually disabled by an engineer for debugging or any other ad hoc purpose.
explanation: POL‑008 §2.2 requires CI/CD traffic to be logged and reviewed weekly; POL‑011 §3.1 prohibits engineers from disabling logging. The prohibition reinforces the logging requirement, so the obligations are complementary.

text_a: All CI/CD pipeline traffic exempted under this policy must be logged and reviewed weekly by the Platform Security team.
text_b: Audit logging (including CloudTrail or equivalent) must remain enabled at all times on all production systems.
explanation: POL‑008 §2.2 specifies logging and weekly review of CI/CD pipeline traffic, while POL‑011 §3.1 requires audit logging to remain enabled at all times on production systems. The two obligations reinforce each other across overlapping scope without conflict.
